title: pH Metric Titration of Acetic Acid vs NaOH
aim: To determine the concentration of a weak acid (CH3COOH) by titrating it against a strong base (NaOH) using a pH meter.
theory: In the titration of a weak acid with a strong base, the pH increases gradually initially. Near the equivalence point, there is a sharp rise in pH. For acetic acid and NaOH, the salt formed (sodium acetate) undergoes basic hydrolysis, making the pH at the equivalence point greater than 7.
procedure: Calibrate the pH meter with pH 4 and pH 7 buffer solutions.
Pipette out 10 mL of acetic acid into a 250 mL beaker.
Add enough distilled water to submerge the pH bulb.
Fill the burette with 0.1M NaOH solution.
Place the beaker on a magnetic stirrer and insert the probe.
Add NaOH in 0.5 mL increments and record the pH.
As the pH starts to rise rapidly, reduce the increment to 0.1 mL.
Continue titration until the pH reaches around 12.
Calculate the equivalence point from the pH vs volume graph.

requiredFormula.description: Where M1 = Molarity of Acid, V1 = Volume of Acid, M2 = Molarity of Base, V2 = Volume of Base at equivalence point.

safety: Acetic acid and NaOH are corrosive.
Handle the pH probe with care (do not touch the glass bulb).
Keep the probe in storage solution when not in use.
